text: The surgeon stands on the patient's left side and uses the traction port to retract the gallbladder fundus towards the patient's right shoulder, providing exposure of the Calot's triangle anatomy. The surgeon's left hand holds the infundibulum.

The surgeon moves the gallbladder in a leg-like manner to expose the common bile duct (CBD) and gallbladder. A burst of energy is applied at the peritoneal fold, clearing fat and enhancing pneumoperitoneum. Posterior dissection of the peritoneum is performed, revealing the liver. Anterior dissection follows, staying close to the gallbladder, allowing identification of the cystic artery, cystic duct, and CBD. The cystic artery is ligated using a ligature, and clips are applied while releasing traction to prevent CBD injury.

The surgeon applies two clips on the body side and one on the specimen side of the gallbladder. Using a ligature, the gallbladder is carefully removed from the liver bed, revealing a thin-walled gallbladder. The procedure is bloodless, with no visible RBCs. The cystic duct is grasped, and its contents, including gallstones, are sectioned out.
